title: Is Exact Markovianity Fundamental Once Time Is Relational?
abstract: Markovian open quantum theory assumes evolution with respect to an external classical time parameter, yet no preferred notion of time exists fundamentally in relativistic physics. We resolve this tension by formulating relativistic open quantum dynamics relationally through finite-resolution quantum clocks. Using the Schwinger-Tomonaga formalism, we derive a covariant master equation directly on spacetime hypersurfaces and show that the resulting reduced dynamics is generically non-Markovian even for local interactions. Environmental correlations and clock fluctuations jointly generate the memory kernel, while the Gorini-Kossakowski-Lindblad-Sudarshan (GKLS) structure emerges only after relational coarse-graining, implying that exact Markovian evolution is effective rather than fundamental. In the sharp-clock limit, the formalism reduces to the Anastopoulos-Hu gravitational decoherence equation.
summary: The paper develops a covariant theory for open quantum dynamics in relativistic settings by treating time as relational, derived from physical quantum clocks. It proves that this relational structure generically leads to non-Markovian evolution, showing that the familiar Markovian master equation is merely an effective approximation valid only under specific coarse-graining assumptions.
main_problem: The paper addresses the fundamental conflict between Markovian open quantum dynamics, which requires an external time parameter, and relativistic physics, where time is fundamentally relational.
main_result: Exact Markovian evolution is shown to be only an effective limit, emerging only after a process of relational coarse-graining; intrinsically, the reduced dynamics are generically non-Markovian due to clock fluctuations.
method: The authors use a covariant formalism based on the Schwinger–Tomonaga equation, developing a time-convolutionless master equation directly on spacetime hypersurfaces by conditioning the evolution on physical quantum clocks.
model_or_system: The model involves a composite relativistic system (System S + Environment B) whose dynamics are governed by local operators on spacelike hypersurfaces $\Sigma$, with time operationalized via a finite-resolution quantum clock C.
key_observables: Reduced density matrix ($ho_{S,	au}$), memory kernel ($C(\sigma)$), and the resulting master equation structure (GKLS vs. non-Markovian).
important_parameters: Clock resolution scale ($\sigma$), environmental correlation time ($	au_B$), and the separation of scales ($\sigma \gg 	au_B$).
main_assumptions: The formalism assumes an operational decomposition into sectors (S, B, C) and relies on approximations like statistical independence between noise sources and separation of scales to recover known limits.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates how finite-resolution clock extraction leads to memory effects from both environmental correlations and clock fluctuations, contrasting this with standard external time dynamics.
paper_structure: The paper builds the covariant formalism using the Schwinger–Tomonaga equation on spacetime hypersurfaces. It derives the reduced relational dynamics, demonstrating non-Markovianity due to the dressed memory kernel $C(\sigma)$. Finally, it shows that the familiar GKLS structure is recovered only in an effective limit via coarse-graining.
why_interesting: This work directly tackles foundational issues at the intersection of quantum gravity and open quantum systems, providing a rigorous framework for how time dependence emerges from physical measurements rather than being imposed externally.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Covariant relational open-system dynamics. A physical observer defines operational time by reading a finite- resolution quantum clock rather than referring to an external classical time parameter. The standing observer on the left represents an operational agent who extracts relational time τ from the quantum clock, whose finite temporal resolution ∆τ introduces intrinsic temporal uncertainty. The subsystem S interacts locally with its environment B on spacetime hypersurfaces Σ, while the total state evolves covariantly according to the Schwinger–Tomonaga equation. Conditioning the dynamics on the clock reading induces a relational reduced evolution in which memory effects arise...
